item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: I updated the WikiWalk post accordingly.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that would work. "Anomalous" legal entities. Because most complex legal entities are perfectly normal things like Apple, Inc., or the federal government of the United States.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Are you saying I should do something like this instead:

+ show block
– hide block

Editing this:

SCP-2553 is an anomalous legal entity.

To this:

SCP-2553 is an anomalous legal entity1.

Footnotes
1. For further examples of complex legal entities, see SCP-1442.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I agree with the concept of linking this article to SCP-1442. But that specific kind of link doesn't work. (For one thing, SCP-1442 doesn't provide more information on complex legal entities, it just happens to be one).

Perhaps it would be better to have the footnote be to the sentence "SCP-2553 is an anomalous legal entity. " at the beginning of the Description section, and then have the footnote read something like "Compare SCP-1442."

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This is a sentient legal entity. This is similar in multiple ways to SCP-1442, which is a sentient corporation. I propose linking the two.

SCP-2553 is an anomalous legal entity.

To this:

SCP-2553 is an anomalous legal entity1.

Footnotes
1. For further examples of anomalous legal entities, see SCP-1442.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also glad to see the return of Sheldon katz

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It /used/ to be its own sole shareholder. Then, once the Foundation and the world's collective legal systems wrangled with it, they fixed that. Now the Foundation controls its sole shareholder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Legal entities definitely can be financially responsible for children, for example as a trust.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the status of legal guardian of several hundred children in the Connecticut state foster system

Are you saying that this sentient legal document is paying child support? How on earth did that happen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted as soon as I successfully parsed "the Ecclesiastical Court of the Russian Orthodox Church for the Diocese of Alaska."

Wanted to upvote several additional times later on. (I fought the law and the law won.)

The last line of the addendum is a wonderful punchline- it goes from terrifying to terrifying-yet-heartwrenching in one sentence.

One question: does SCP-2553 have a name? Usually when an SCP has a preexisting name or designation, that name is present in the article (though sometimes only as black boxes) but here… the thing is extensively documented, it was identified by the Foundation somehow and lots of information has been collected from different sources and verified as referring to it, and yet there's no mention of it having a name anywhere. Presumably all these legal documents don't say "SCP-2553" on them, especially the ones from before the Foundation discovered the entity…

If it doesn't have a name (or other identifier) that's consistent across all of its paperwork- for instance, if it exclusively refers to itself by descriptors like "affiant"- how do we know that all the paperwork refers to the same entity? If there were lots of them- for instance, if the one that filed the DNR orders was a different entity than the one that purchased the school buses- how would we tell?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted when I got to the yellow fringe on the flag. It's an honest-to-god Sovereign Citizen! I love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
PLEASE DONT HURT ME
Too good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for those links, Procyon. They made for excellent reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We can dissolve you, we can revoke your charter, and we can tax you.

This sentence is equal parts badass, funny, and horrifying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What a wonderful entity.
I imagine it could keep the staff quite busy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Many of the SCP objects are creepy, weird and unsettling. This is the only one that has truly frightened me. Sentient legal documentation that exists as a corporate entity. The ramifications of this are mind-numbingly terrifying. Granted, the power that corporations wield over the everyday lives of people is one of the most horrifying things in the world to me, so…yeah.

In the words of a famous (fictional) sea captain: "Fetch my brown pants."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last line made me terrified. In a strongly Kafkian sense.

This entity was created by legal proceedings. It may not exist in any other way. It's sentient, we know that through the Addendum. And it has the Foundation containing it so efficiently, so perfectly, it's scared.

Just what laws could the Foundation pass, if they wanted to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…the prohibition of zeppelins from entering Moldovan air space…

This is one of the most inspired phrases I have ever read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because Sheldon Katz being badass lawyer. Again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Had a bit of difficulty wading through the legalese, but I rather liked the ending interview and the way you presented his dialogue.

Also, nice to see Sheldon Katz of SCP-738 come around again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this. Original, entertaining, funny, and even a bit scary (because I live in Canada). I'm not sure I quite…get it, though. I think it could have been longer, perhaps. Weak upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought that name was familiar, but I couldn't quite put my finger on it until I looked it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic. For once, I am duly pleased to know the origins of a skip. This is a near perfect article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the fact that it took a filing from a sovereign citizen to create such a singularity of legal illogic, that a sentient legal entity was the result. Those people be cra-a-azy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I adore everything about this.

I want to see a tale about 2553's dealings with The Republic of Arnold Fitzwilliams.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is Sui Generis. Absolutely fantastic. Just as an example, this final aside:
"and the entry of Do Not Resuscitate orders for every hospital patient in Canada"

Really excellent. Im still thinking about it a couple hours later.
=D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found myself struggling with a fair amount of the terminology (complex multijurisdictional commercial tax litigation proceeding) and was unable to really figure out what the article was all about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the fringe is a clever little Easter egg, actually. There are people who argue that a decision in court doesn't apply to them because of the existence of the fringe (they'll say it's a naval banner and not an official national flag, and that means they can ignore the court, which, no).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral vote.

A lot of the content in here doesn't make any sense, what does the fringe on the flag matter? Why do you need notaries around to guard a certificate of origin? Why does the location of the certificate of origin even matter? If you can clearly just perform operation of questionable legality (how you contend you forced a merger between a shell corporation and this SCP when the SCP was the sole shareholder in the SCP is beyond imagination) why don't you just strip it of all legal rights its accumulated? Why exactly does the SCP amend that confidential document to allow an interview? This thing exists exclusively in the legal system? The article didn't sell me on that you've laid out how that would work.

The entire addendum is a huge drag on the article, and the writing style you chose (intentionally shit legalese) although apt to what the SCP should sound like, is still not at all entertaining to read, and whats more that simpering line at the end almost made me groan.

The idea could be cool but it needs way more than a spit shine to get to an interesting point IMO, there are too many logically inconsistent points for me to endorse this (I'm sure someone with a more background on the technical side of this could point out a host more issues/overlooked concepts)

This seems to be being well received so it is likely this just isn't my type of article but I think this needs a lot of fixing before it would get my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SHELDON KATZ!

So awesome to see him crop up again.

I was thinking of doing something similar with a poorly and polymorphically worded end user license agreement, but I might have to hold off on it, now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
PLEASE DON'T HURT ME I'M AFRAID

Actually laughed out loud at this. +1. It is a bit containment-focused, as all of spike's articles tend to be, but it works pretty well here since the stuff in the containment procedures and in the main description both get interesting payoff in the addendum. The legalese is complicated enough to sell the complexity of the entity without being so dense as to lose the reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First time I saw an anomalous legal entity, and it is detailed and enjoyable to the end. Especially the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a lawyer, this is very fun, although since it's its own sole shareholder, it should be able to mount a shareholder representative action to injunct those articles of dissolution. Perhaps sue the directors for breach of fiduciary duty. Anyway +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the addendum. It made me laugh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is good, though I also believe that the creation is absurd. In addition, I have issues with the name dropping of various organisations.
If approval is required, that should get routed through the GOC.

Borderline neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) My sense of humor was shot off in the war.
2) An arcane process may be expected to summon or create a sentient entity. As can a deliberate techlogical attempt to do so. Microwaving non-dairy creamer probably can't.
3) If random legal errors can create sapience a) how is then an anomaly, since it is then totally mundane in origin and b) why aren't sapient legal constructs running around since Hammurabi.

Anyway, this whole drift is pointless. I like the scip. I find the proposed origin to be not funny and out of place. You aren't going to convince me. I'm not going to convince you. 'nuff said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is somewhat similar to a previous sentient corporation (SCP-1442) but is different enough to get a pass on that. That one is an active company whose physical elements (humans and materials) unconsciously act in concert to create a sentient mind.
This one is a nebulous legal entity with no actual physical assets (human or material) that acts on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since when do skips makes sense? Besides, there are plenty of other skips that were created through arcane rituals with impossible-to-understand incantations. What is legalese but a system of arcane rituals with impossible-to-understand incantations?

P.S. Even if the origin premise is absurd, I, personally, do not find that an element of absurdity automatically relegates something to the -J set. If an absurd premise is treated seriously (see: Moon Snakes), it can make a decent mainlister.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The second point of the third footnote has a left bracket without a pair.

Anyhow, loved it. This sounds mindnumbingly complex to write.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It does have something to do with stories, which this is. Just saying "but law is weird" is kind of a cop-out, like "radiation!", IMO, YMMV.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since when did logic have anything to do with the legal profession?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No doubt, but none of them can logically result in sapience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Trust me, there are some weird as hell pro se filings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. I love this even though it has one niggling bit1 that bugs me, and that takes work!

Footnotes
1. Its supposed origin through mundane legal error, is completely absurd and this isn't a -J skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A very good execution of a fun idea.
+1
